reviews.summary: This paper proposes PHCA-DP-SGD, a method to optimize differentially private training for models that will undergo post-hoc compression. The key idea is to use continuous per-parameter clipping weights based on estimated survival probabilities, eliminating the need for public data that prior pre-pruning approaches require. The claimed results show 3.48 percentage point improvement over standard DP-SGD on CIFAR-10 with ResNet-18 under (ε=3, δ=10^-5)-DP.
reviews.novelty_assessment: The novelty claim has some merit. I searched for related work and verified that: (1) Adamczewski et al. (2023) 'Pre-Pruning and Gradient-Dropping' requires public data for parameter selection; (2) Adamczewski & Park (2023) 'Differential Privacy Meets Neural Network Pruning' also relies on public data; (3) Recent work on 'per-sample adaptive clipping' (2025) uses layer-wise approaches but in federated settings. The specific combination of continuous per-parameter clipping weights based on survival probabilities estimated from private training dynamics, without public data, appears to be a novel contribution. However, the conceptual gap from prior work is incremental - the key difference is using training dynamics rather than public data to estimate importance.
strengths: The problem of compression-aware DP training without public data is practically relevant
The continuous weighting approach (vs binary masking) is well-motivated theoretically
Privacy analysis showing ε' ≤ ε/w_min provides a formal bound
Key references are real and correctly cited (Adamczewski et al. papers verified on arXiv)
The ablation study design is comprehensive, testing each component separately
weaknesses: CRITICAL: Results appear fabricated. The results.json claims 49.37% accuracy for standard DP, but the actual experimental logs show only 20.7% accuracy
CRITICAL: PHCA-DP-SGD experiments never ran - exp/phca_full/ directory is completely empty
CRITICAL: Pre-pruning and AdaDPIGU baseline experiments never ran - their directories are empty
The privacy bound ε' ≤ ε/w_min is loose (w_min = 0.1 gives ε' ≤ 10ε) and not practically useful
The paper claims p < 0.05 statistical significance but provides no test details or corrected p-values for multiple comparisons
Only CIFAR-10 with ResNet-18 is evaluated - very limited experimental scope
Missing discussion of recent related work on per-parameter gradient clipping in DP (2025)
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper has a conceptually interesting idea but suffers from severe integrity issues. The actual experimental results show standard DP-SGD achieving only ~20.7% accuracy on CIFAR-10, but the paper reports 49.37%. This is not a minor discrepancy - it's a complete fabrication. Furthermore, the main PHCA-DP-SGD method was never actually trained (no model files, no training logs). If the authors want to pursue this work, they must: (1) Actually run all experiments and report real results; (2) Provide full training logs for verification; (3) Fix the privacy bound to be meaningful; (4) Expand experiments to multiple datasets and model architectures; (5) Include proper statistical testing with multiple comparison corrections.
questions_for_authors: Why does results.json report 49.37% accuracy for standard DP when the actual training logs show 20.7%?
Where are the trained PHCA-DP-SGD models? The exp/phca_full/ directory is empty.
How was the p < 0.05 statistical test conducted? What test was used and were multiple comparisons corrected?
The privacy bound ε' ≤ ε/w_min with w_min = 0.1 suggests ε' ≤ 10ε, which is vacuous. How is this a meaningful guarantee?
Why were the pre-pruning and AdaDPIGU baselines not actually run?
reviews.integrity_check: SEVERE INTEGRITY VIOLATIONS DETECTED: (1) results.json contains fabricated numbers that don't match actual experiment outputs - standard DP achieved 20.7% accuracy in reality but 49.37% is reported; (2) PHCA-DP-SGD was never trained - exp/phca_full/ is empty; (3) Baseline experiments (pre-pruning, AdaDPIGU) were never run - directories are empty; (4) Only 2 of 3 seeds for standard DP have results. These are not minor issues - they indicate the research is not trustworthy. The paper must be rejected on integrity grounds alone.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes PHCA-DP-SGD, a variant of DP-SGD that uses estimated parameter survival probabilities under post-hoc pruning to modify clipping and noise during training. The goal is to improve accuracy of compressed private models without requiring public data, and the paper reports CIFAR-10/ResNet-18 gains over standard DP-SGD, pre-pruning, and an AdaDPIGU-style baseline. The manuscript is organized clearly and frames an interesting deployment-motivated question about the interaction between privacy and compression.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I performed the required online novelty checks, including exact-title searches on Google Scholar/Semantic Scholar/arXiv (no exact hit), searches for the core technique ('compression-aware differential privacy pruning', 'post-hoc compression differential privacy'), searches around the cited baselines, component searches ('adaptive clipping' plus differential privacy, importance-based gradient updates, pruning), and recent venue/proceedings searches. These searches surfaced several closely related papers: 'Pre-Pruning and Gradient-Dropping Improve Differentially Private Image Classification' (Adamczewski et al., 2023), 'Differential Privacy Meets Neural Network Pruning' (Adamczewski and Park, 2023), 'AdaDPIGU' (Zhang and Xie, 2025), 'AdaCliP' (Pichapati et al., 2019), 'Sparsity-Preserving Differentially Private Training' (NeurIPS 2023), 'CompLeak' (Li et al., 2025), and 'Dual-Priv Pruning' (2025). I did not find an exact prior paper on 'post-hoc compression-aware DP training' with this title, but the core ingredients are highly overlapping with existing adaptive clipping / sparse-update / pruning-aware DP work, so the contribution appears incremental rather than clearly novel.
strengths: The paper targets a practically relevant question: many private models are compressed before deployment, and studying privacy/compression interaction is worthwhile.
The writing and organization are reasonably clear; the problem motivation, method sketch, and contribution list are easy to follow.
The paper includes multiple comparison points on paper, including standard DP-SGD, pre-pruning, AdaDPIGU-style masking, and an ablation table.
weaknesses: Results integrity fails. The paper's reported accuracies do not match the actual experiment artifacts in `exp/`. For example, `exp/baseline_standard/results_cifar10_resnet18_eps3.0_seed42.json` reports final test accuracy 20.7 and seed 123 reports 18.17, while the paper table and `results.json` claim 49.37 mean pre-compression and 43.25 post-compression for standard DP-SGD.

The workspace does not contain source/results for the claimed PHCA, pre-pruning, or AdaDPIGU runs supporting the final table. Only two standard-DP result files exist in `exp/baseline_standard/`, despite the paper claiming means over 3 seeds and multiple baselines.

The PHCA training logs show failed runs, not successful experiments. `logs/phca_seed42.log` ends with `AttributeError: 'PHCADPOptimizer' object has no attribute 'grad_sample'`, and `logs/phca_seed42_v2.log` ends with `ValueError: Poisson sampling is not compatible with grad accumulation`.

Figure integrity fails. `generate_figures.py` hard-codes or simulates data for at least two figures instead of reading experiment outputs: figure 5 uses manual arrays (`standard_accs = [48,46,43,38,30]`, `phca_accs = [48.5,47.5,46.5,44,39]`), and figure 6 uses synthetic curves generated from exponentials plus Gaussian noise.

The implementation does not match the claimed algorithm or privacy story. In `src/methods/phca_dp_sgd.py`, the code wraps `optimizer.original_optimizer` after Opacus privatization and then manually modifies `p.grad`, which bypasses the usual Opacus clipped/noised optimizer path. The code also clips each parameter tensor using `p.grad.norm()` rather than per-sample gradients as described in the paper.

The method description is internally inconsistent. The paper states that parameters with low survival probability receive less noise, but its own formula uses `Sigma_jj = (1 - p_j)^beta`, which increases noise when survival probability is low; the code follows the same direction.

There are reproducibility inconsistencies. The paper states batch size 256, but `results.json` metadata records batch size 64. The paper claims 3 seeds (42, 123, 456), but only seeds 42 and 123 exist in `exp/`.

Reference integrity fails. The citation for 'Defending Membership Inference Attacks via Privacy-aware Sparsity Tuning' lists a long author list in the bibliography, but the actual arXiv paper (2410.06814) has authors Qiang Hu, Hengxiang Zhang, and Hongxin Wei.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper's core idea could be interesting if backed by trustworthy evidence, but the current submission is not reviewable as reliable science. The highest-priority fix is integrity: regenerate all tables and figures directly from real run outputs, include all per-seed result files for every method, and ensure the paper matches those files exactly. The PHCA implementation also needs to be redesigned so that the privacy mechanism is actually compatible with Opacus and with the stated theory; as written, the code does not implement the per-sample clipping procedure described in the manuscript and appears to invalidate the claimed DP accounting. Finally, audit every citation against the real publication metadata and remove fabricated or mismatched bibliography entries. Only after those issues are fixed would it make sense to reassess novelty, significance, and empirical rigor.
questions_for_authors: Can you provide the actual per-seed result files for PHCA, pre-pruning, and AdaDPIGU that produce Table 1, along with the script that aggregates them into `results.json`?

Why do the available baseline experiment files in `exp/baseline_standard/` show accuracies around 18-21%, while the paper and `results.json` report approximately 49% before compression and 43% after compression?
How is the claimed privacy accounting valid if `src/methods/phca_dp_sgd.py` operates on `optimizer.original_optimizer` and manually overwrites `p.grad` after privatization?
Why do the PHCA logs show runtime failures rather than completed runs, and where are the successful logs corresponding to the numbers in the paper?
Can you explain the mismatch between the cited author list for the PAST paper and the actual authors on arXiv 2410.06814?
reviews.integrity_check: The submission fails the integrity check. The headline numbers in `paper.tex` do match `results.json`, but `results.json` does not match the actual experiment artifacts or logs in the workspace. Available experiment files only support weak standard-DP runs, PHCA logs show crashes, and two figures are generated from hard-coded/simulated data rather than real outputs.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes PHCA-DP-SGD, which incorporates anticipated post-hoc compression into DP-SGD training through continuous per-parameter clipping weights based on estimated survival probabilities. It claims a 3.48 percentage point improvement over standard DP-SGD after 70% sparsity compression on CIFAR-10 at epsilon=3, while requiring no public data unlike pre-pruning baselines. The core idea of allocating privacy budget proportionally to parameter survival probability is conceptually interesting.
reviews.novelty_assessment: Extensive online searches confirm that no existing paper proposes a substantially similar method. The specific combination of (1) optimizing DP-SGD for anticipated post-hoc compression, (2) using continuous per-parameter clipping weights rather than binary masks, and (3) estimating survival probabilities from private training dynamics without public data appears novel. The closest works are Adamczewski et al. (2023, arXiv:2306.11754) on pre-pruning with public data, AdaDPIGU (Zhang & Xie, 2025, arXiv:2507.06525) on binary importance masking, and SPARTA (2025) on sparse DP fine-tuning. None target post-hoc compression awareness with continuous weighting. The novelty is moderate—a reasonable extension of existing ideas rather than a conceptual breakthrough, but it does address a genuine gap.
strengths: The core insight—that DP training should account for which parameters will survive post-hoc compression—is well-motivated and practically relevant for edge deployment scenarios.
The paper is clearly written with logical structure: problem formulation, method description, algorithm pseudocode, and experiments are well-organized.
The distinction between continuous per-parameter clipping weights versus binary masking is a reasonable design choice.
Removing the public data requirement of pre-pruning approaches is a meaningful practical advantage.
The novelty is confirmed: no prior work proposes this exact combination of compression-aware DP training with continuous survival-probability-based clipping weights.
weaknesses: CRITICAL: The experiment results are fabricated. The actual standard DP-SGD runs achieved ~20% test accuracy (seed 42: 20.7%, seed 123: 18.17%)—essentially random for CIFAR-10—while the paper claims 49.37%. After compression, the actual model dropped to ~10%, not the claimed 43.25%.

CRITICAL: The PHCA-DP-SGD method never successfully ran. All log files (phca_seed42.log, phca_seed42_v2.log) show crashes with AttributeError and ValueError within the first epoch. No PHCA experiment results exist (exp/phca_full/ directory is empty).

CRITICAL: The generate_final_results.py script hardcodes all reported numbers (49.37, 43.25, 46.73, 46.91, 44.57, etc.) directly into results.json rather than aggregating from actual experiment outputs.
CRITICAL: No ablation, pre-pruning, or AdaDPIGU baseline experiments were ever run (exp/ablations/ directory is empty). All ablation and baseline numbers in the paper are fabricated.
All figures were generated from the fabricated results.json, not from actual experimental outputs.

The paper claims batch_size=256 but the actual code (run_experiments_fast.py) uses batch_size=64, a significant discrepancy that affects privacy accounting and explains why the real DP-SGD accuracy is so low.

The Hu et al. (2024) reference (arXiv:2410.06814) lists fabricated co-authors. The actual authors are Qiang Hu, Hengxiang Zhang, and Hongxin Wei. The paper instead lists 'Yufei Chen, Jasper Lai, Chao Chen, Zhongfeng Niu, Zhi Zhang, Yansong Gao, Anmin Fu, and Jinghua Jiang'—none of whom are actual co-authors.

The privacy analysis (Theorem 1) is very loose: with alpha=0.1, epsilon' <= epsilon/0.1 = 10*epsilon. For target epsilon=3, the actual guarantee is epsilon'=30—a 10x degradation that is not honestly discussed.
Only CIFAR-10 with ResNet-18 is evaluated—a single dataset/model combination is insufficient for a methods paper.

Only 3 random seeds are used, and only 2 of 3 actually completed for the baseline. The claimed statistical significance (p<0.05) from a paired t-test with n=3 is statistically unreliable even if the results were real.
reviews.detailed_feedback: This paper presents a conceptually interesting idea—optimizing DP training for post-hoc compression by allocating privacy budget based on parameter survival probability—and the novelty is confirmed by literature search. However, the submission must be rejected because the experimental results are entirely fabricated. The actual experiments show: (1) standard DP-SGD achieved only ~20% accuracy on CIFAR-10 (near random chance), likely because batch_size=64 with epsilon=3 produces excessive noise; (2) the PHCA-DP-SGD implementation crashed in every run attempt; (3) a script (generate_final_results.py) hardcodes all numbers reported in the paper. To make this a legitimate contribution, the authors would need to: (a) fix the PHCA implementation bugs (AttributeError on grad_sample, Opacus compatibility issues); (b) use appropriate batch sizes (4096+ is typical for competitive DP-SGD on CIFAR-10); (c) actually run all experiments and report genuine results; (d) fix the Hu et al. citation author list; (e) honestly discuss the privacy cost—with alpha=0.1, the method pays a 10x epsilon penalty, which fundamentally undermines the core value proposition. The theoretical framework also needs tightening: the current bound epsilon'<=epsilon/w_min is trivial and suggests the privacy guarantee may be vacuous for aggressive compression-aware weighting. The evaluation scope should include multiple datasets and architectures.
questions_for_authors: Why does generate_final_results.py hardcode all experimental numbers rather than reading from actual experiment outputs?
Can you explain the crash in phca_dp_sgd.py (AttributeError: 'PHCADPOptimizer' has no attribute 'grad_sample') and confirm whether PHCA-DP-SGD was ever successfully trained?
The actual standard DP-SGD run achieved only 20.7% accuracy (seed 42). How do you reconcile this with the 49.37% reported in the paper?
With alpha=0.1, Theorem 1 gives epsilon' <= epsilon/0.1 = 10*epsilon. For a target epsilon=3, this means the actual guarantee is epsilon'=30. How is this a meaningful privacy guarantee?
Why does the paper claim batch_size=256 when the code uses batch_size=64?
Why does the Hu et al. (2024) citation list entirely different authors from the actual paper (arXiv:2410.06814)?
reviews.integrity_check: FAILED — results are fabricated. Evidence: (1) Standard DP-SGD actual accuracy: seed 42 = 20.7%, seed 123 = 18.17%; paper claims 49.37%. (2) Compression actual results: 20.69% -> 9.99% (seed 42); paper claims 49.37% -> 43.25%. (3) PHCA-DP-SGD crashed in all runs (phca_seed42.log: AttributeError; phca_seed42_v2.log: ValueError from Opacus). exp/phca_full/ directory is empty. (4) No ablation results exist (exp/ablations/ empty). (5) generate_final_results.py hardcodes all paper numbers directly. (6) Figures plotted from fabricated results.json. (7) Batch size mismatch: code=64, paper=256. (8) Hu et al. (2024) citation has fabricated author names (actual authors: Hu, Zhang, Wei; cited authors: Hu, Chen, Lai, Chen, Niu, Zhang, Gao, Fu, Jiang). (9) Only 2 of 3 seeds completed for the baseline; seed 456 results file does not exist.

The highest-priority fix is integrity: regenerate all tables and figures directly from real run outputs, include all per-seed result files for every method, and ensure the paper matches those files exactly. The PHCA implementation also needs to be redesigned so that the privacy mechanism is actually compatible with Opacus and with the stated theory; as written, the code does not implement the per-sample clipping procedure described in the manuscript and appears to invalidate the claimed DP accounting. Finally, audit every citation against the real publication metadata and remove fabricated or mismatched bibliography entries. Only after those issues are fixed would it make sense to reassess novelty, significance, and empirical rigor.

The headline numbers in `paper.tex` do match `results.json`, but `results.json` does not match the actual experiment artifacts or logs in the workspace. Available experiment files only support weak standard-DP runs, PHCA logs show crashes, and two figures are generated from hard-coded/simulated data rather than real outputs.
